repository.name: express
repository.description: Fast, unopinionated, minimalist web framework for node.

disclosures: Architectural relationships are inferred from static analysis and directory structure — runtime behavior may differ.
Debt signals are indicators of maintenance risk, not definitive proof of technical debt.
History may be sampled; generated, binary, vendored and unsupported files are excluded.
Pre-analyzed snapshot of the real repository, captured 2026-07-22 by the CodeChronicle worker. The live repository has moved on since.
Author identities are derived from commit emails, not GitHub accounts.

debtSignals.title: Dependency concentration around hub modules
debtSignals.description: test/acceptance is connected to 19+ modules — changes there ripple widely.
debtSignals.methodology: Module in/out-degree on the latest import graph; flagged at ≥2.5× the mean degree (mean: 2.0).

debtSignals.title: 3 oversized source files
debtSignals.description: lib/response.js is 1,051 lines and among the most-edited files. Large files tend to accumulate mixed responsibilities.
debtSignals.methodology: Line count per source file in the most recent tree snapshot; threshold 450 lines (excludes tests, vendored and generated files).

debtSignals.title: 20 reverts in analyzed history
debtSignals.description: Frequent reverts suggest changes ship before their impact is understood.

refactorOpportunities.title: Reduce the blast radius of test/acceptance
evidence: test/acceptance is connected to 19+ modules — changes there ripple widely.
Detected via: Module in/out-degree on the latest import graph; flagged at ≥2.5× the mean degree (mean: 2.0).
refactorOpportunities.benefit: Fewer modules recompile, retest and break when the hub changes.
refactorOpportunities.risk: Splitting a hub that is genuinely cohesive adds indirection without payoff — verify the hub mixes concerns first.
refactorOpportunities.firstStep: List the distinct reasons other modules import test/acceptance; if there are ≥2 unrelated reasons, split along that line.

refactorOpportunities.title: Split lib/response.js
evidence: lib/response.js is 1,051 lines and among the most-edited files. Large files tend to accumulate mixed responsibilities.
Detected via: Line count per source file in the most recent tree snapshot; threshold 450 lines (excludes tests, vendored and generated files).
refactorOpportunities.benefit: Smaller units localize changes, shrink review surface and reduce merge conflicts.
refactorOpportunities.risk: Mechanical splitting without a responsibility boundary just spreads the tangle across files.
refactorOpportunities.firstStep: Group lib/response.js's top-level declarations by the data they touch; move the most independent group out first.

refactorOpportunities.title: Add tests to high-churn, low-test files
evidence: 6 frequently-changed source files never change together with a test file (lib/application.js, lib/request.js, lib/express.js).
Co-change with tests is a proxy: these files may still be covered indirectly.
refactorOpportunities.benefit: The files most likely to change next gain a safety net exactly where regressions are most likely.
refactorOpportunities.risk: Retro-fitted tests can freeze current behavior, bugs included — write them against intent, not implementation.
refactorOpportunities.firstStep: Start with lib/application.js: cover its most recently changed code path first.
